title: Anomalous tunneling as a low-energy theorem for Nambu-Goldstone modes
authors: Keisuke Fujii, Daichi Kagamihara, Masaru Hongo
abstract: Anomalous tunneling refers to the phenomenon in which the transmission coefficient through a potential barrier approaches unity as the energy of an incident particle or quasiparticle tends to zero. This counterintuitive effect has been reported in systems exhibiting spontaneous symmetry breaking (SSB), such as superfluids, yet the general conditions for its occurrence remain unclear. In this Letter, we establish that anomalous tunneling of Nambu-Goldstone (NG) modes is a universal low-energy theorem dictated solely by symmetry and scaling, using a low-energy effective field theory (EFT) framework. We formulate the scattering of NG modes by external potentials in terms of spatially dependent EFT coefficients and demonstrate that symmetry-preserving localized potentials are irrelevant in the long-wavelength limit, leading to perfect transmission. In contrast, symmetry-breaking perturbations are relevant and suppress transmission, resulting in the absence of anomalous tunneling. We illustrate this universal behavior with explicit examples of superfluid phonons and magnons.
summary: This paper establishes that the perfect transmission of Nambu-Goldstone modes through potential barriers at zero energy is a universal consequence of symmetry and scaling. It demonstrates that while symmetry-preserving potentials do not impede transmission in the long-wavelength limit, symmetry-breaking perturbations suppress it. This provides a general theoretical foundation for anomalous tunneling in various broken-symmetry phases.
main_problem: The paper seeks to establish the general conditions under which anomalous tunneling (perfect transmission at zero energy) occurs in systems with spontaneous symmetry breaking.
main_result: The authors prove that anomalous tunneling of Nambu-Goldstone modes is a universal low-energy theorem determined by symmetry and scaling, where symmetry-preserving potentials are irrelevant in the long-wavelength limit.
method: The study utilizes a low-energy effective field theory (EFT) framework to formulate the scattering of Nambu-Goldstone modes by external potentials.
model_or_system: The framework applies to Nambu-Goldstone modes in systems with spontaneous symmetry breaking, with specific illustrative examples provided for superfluid phonons and magnons.
key_observables: Transmission coefficient as a function of incident particle/quasiparticle energy.
important_parameters: Low-energy/long-wavelength limit, symmetry-preserving vs. symmetry-breaking perturbations, and spatially dependent EFT coefficients.
main_assumptions: The derivation assumes the validity of the low-energy effective field theory and focuses on the long-wavelength limit.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of anomalous tunneling, establishes a universal theorem using EFT, distinguishes between relevant and irrelevant perturbations based on symmetry, and provides specific examples (phonons and magnons).
why_interesting: It provides a fundamental, symmetry-based principle for understanding transport properties of collective excitations in many-body systems, which is highly relevant to the dynamics of ultracold gases and superfluids.
